# Introduction

This project compares the mobility behaviour of two individuals using two different tracking methods: a GPS logger app for Christopher and Google Timeline @GoogleTimeline data for Amelia. The aim is to analyse how personal movement patterns can be described from timestamped geographic coordinates and how the choice of tracking method affects data quality and processing effort.

Descriptive spatio-temporal trajectory analysis was used to calculate movement indicators such as travelled distance, average speed, time spent away from home, commuting time to ZHAW and maximum distance from home. In addition, the trajectories were matched with OpenStreetMap road network data to estimate which road types were used most frequently. The interpretation of the results was supported by the participants’ own knowledge of their daily movements.
Furthermore, the project compares how GPS Logger and Google Timeline data differ in accuracy, preprocessing effort and reliability for mobility analysis.
